notable.notable_blurb: Gilded Age railroad magnate and robber baron

bio.bio_blurb: Gilded Age railroad financier and speculator

narrative_blocks.header: Wall Street’s most hated robber baron
narrative_blocks.body: A self-made speculator in railroads and telegraphs, Jay Gould helped trigger the Black Friday gold panic of 1869 and built one of the Gilded Age’s great fortunes — earning a reputation as the era’s most ruthless financier.

narrative_blocks.header: He tried to corner the entire gold market
narrative_blocks.body: In 1869 Jay Gould and James Fisk schemed to corner the nation’s gold supply; when the Treasury released gold on September 24, the resulting crash became known as Black Friday and ruined scores of speculators. The episode sealed his reputation as the era’s most feared financier.

notable.notable_blurb: Financier and railroad executive; son of Jay Gould

cross_connections.display_label: the Wainwright who married his daughter Edith

bio.bio_blurb: Jay Gould’s eldest son and railroad heir

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited an empire and slowly lost it
narrative_blocks.body: Eldest son and chief heir of Jay Gould, George ran the family’s transcontinental railroad system at the turn of the century, only to see rivals led by E. H. Harriman dismantle much of it.

notable.notable_blurb: Stage actress who married George Jay Gould

cross_connections.display_label: the Wainwright who married her daughter Edith

bio.bio_blurb: Broadway actress who married George Jay Gould

narrative_blocks.header: She died on her own private golf course
narrative_blocks.body: A Broadway actress before her marriage, Edith Kingdon Gould left the stage for the Gould fortune and the vast Georgian Court estate in New Jersey. She died suddenly of a heart attack there in 1921 while playing a round of golf on the family links.

bio.bio_blurb: Gould heiress and writer; later Lady MacNeal

narrative_blocks.header: Born on a yacht into the Gould fortune
narrative_blocks.body: A granddaughter of robber baron Jay Gould, Edith was born aboard her father’s yacht and eloped at eighteen — hours after graduating from Miss Spence’s — with the artist Carroll Wainwright. She later wrote a candid memoir of growing up a Gould.

narrative_blocks.header: She divorced a Wainwright and became a Lady
narrative_blocks.body: After her 1932 divorce from the painter Carroll Wainwright, Edith Catherine Gould married Sir Hector MacNeal and took the title Lady MacNeal. She died of pneumonia in 1937, only thirty-six years old.

burialCemetery.name: Woodlawn Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic garden cemetery, Bronx NY. Burial site of T01463 James Frederick Talcott (Cypress Plot, Sec 47, Grave 1) and I00193 Louise Simmons Talcott.
